0: <title>Comparative life cycle assessment of rex rabbit breeding industry chains: benefits of a circular industry chain </title>
Heda Wang 1,2,3,4 Jing Liu ¹, 2,3,4 Jinhua Li 1,2,3,4 Zhibin Jia 1,2,3,4 Chonglei Li 1,2,3,4

Received: 1 October 2020 / Accepted: 27 February 2022 / Published online: 16 March 2022 © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2022, corrected publication 2022
1: Purpose The environmental impacts of breeding industries have been broadly studied and compared. However, environmental research on the rex rabbit breeding industry is scarce. The aim of this study was to evaluate the environmental impacts of two rex rabbit industry chains and verify which one has better environmental benefits, provide a scientific reference for the construction of a circular industry chain, and promote sustainable development of rex rabbit breeding and related industries. Methods Two different rex rabbit industry chains were considered: scenario 1 (a traditional industry chain, including feed cultivation, feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, and slaughtering stages); and scenario 2 (a circular industry chain, includ- ing feed cultivation, feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, slaughtering, manure treatment, and biogas power generation stages). A comparative study was performed using the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology. The functional unit of this study was an average rabbit during 140 days of breeding. A transparent cradle-to-gate life cycle inventory of the two scenarios was provided through the field investigation of a rex rabbit enterprise in China, and SimaPro8.0.4 software was used to conduct the comparative LCA. The CML 2001-Nov.2010 method was used for characterization and normalization of the environmental impacts.

Results and Discussion The environmental performance of S2 was better than that of S1 with a significant reduction in each impact category, except for acidification potential (AP). Terrestric ecotoxicity potential (TETP) and freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity potential (FAETP) were the primary environmental impact categories for S1, mainly because of electricity and fertilizer consumption. TETP and FAETP of S2, when compared with S1, were reduced by 93.7% and 91.6%, respectively. AP was the primary environmental impact category for S2. The largest contributor to each impact category was found in the feed cultivation stage; however, most of the impact categories of the feed cultivation stage were reduced in S2 when com- pared with S1, mainly because of the extension of the industry chain and reuse of byproducts of the manure treatment stage. Conclusions Comparative LCA of the two scenarios confirmed the environmental feasibility and benefits of the circular rex rabbit industry chain. By establishing the circular industry chain instead of the traditional industry chain and increas- ing the waste reuse and recycling to generate clean energy and reduce the consumption of chemical fertilizers, we can not only reduce the overall environmental impacts but also increase the environmental benefits of the rex rabbit industry chain.
Keywords Rex rabbit breeding industry . Life cycle assessment (LCA) . Circular industry chain . Scenario analysis Environmental impact . Benefit analysis
Heda Wang and Jing Liu contributed equally to this work.

4: By 2075, the world human population is predicted to reach 9.2 billion (Alexandratos and Bruinsma 2012). Therefore, the agriculture sector needs to enhance its efficiency to feed the population (Crosson et al. 2011). However, con- sumers have shown an important evolution towards high- quality food produced in environment-friendly conditions (de Boer 2003; Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014; Iribarren et al. 2011; Sala et al. 2017).

Rabbits have extremely high economic and practical value, and rabbit meat has great nutritional value and dietetic properties; it is characterized by high protein content and low fat content (Cesari et al. 2018). Rabbit fur has characteristics such as shortness, fitness, density, smoothness, and beauty (Tao 1994). The rex rabbit yields both meat and fur; it is suitable for breeding in China, as its feeding range covers many provinces. The rex rabbit breeding industry plays an important role in improving income and driving local development (Yang and Hong- Jun 2010).

With the development of the rex rabbit industry and increasing quantity and quality requirements of domestic and international markets for rex rabbit products, China's rex rabbit breeding industry has developed from traditional feeding to large-scale, industrialized, specialized, and environment-friendly breeding and processing. Nowadays, conventional rex rabbit production has three main stages: breeding, slaughtering, and manure treatment. Rex rab- bits are bred and raised until they gain the desired weight. Then, they are sent to the slaughterhouse to collect their meat and skin. Manure is considered a byproduct; rab- bit manure and urine are high-quality organic fertilizers. An adult rabbit can yield more than 100 kg of fertilizer per year, and 10 adult rabbits provide fertilizer equivalent to that yielded by a pig (Istikomah 2011). The nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium contents in rabbit manure are higher than those in the manure of other domestic animals. Rabbit manure contains about 7.7 times, 3.8 times, and 3.3 times more nitrogen than pig, sheep, and chicken manure, respectively (Istikomah 2011). Every 100 kg of rabbit manure is equivalent to 10.85 kg of ammonium sulfate, 10.85 kg of ammonium superphosphate, 10.90 kg of cal- cium superphosphate, and 1.79 kg of potassium superacid (Wu et al. 2010).

However, rapid expansion of the rex rabbit industry has led to a disordered increase in the number of farms and extensive breeding patterns, resulting in poor quality (Pei et al. 2012). Each link of the rex rabbit industry chain also requires large amounts of energy and raw materials, lead- ing to environmental impacts (López-Andrés et al. 2018). The breeding of rex rabbits may cause serious wastage
of valuable resources and environmental pollution (Xiong and Zhu 2007). Therefore, it is necessary to assess the environmental impacts of the rex rabbit breeding industry.

Different methods have been developed to evaluate the environmental impacts of products or production systems. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is one of the most common methods used to analyze environmental impacts (Nwe et al. 2010; Roy et al. 2009). It is a standardized and holistic meth- odology that allows for the identification of the environmen- tal consequences of the life cycle of a product by evaluating potential environmental impacts over the entire life cycle production chain. Many researchers have used LCA to evalu- ate a wide range of breeding industries (Roy et al. 2009).

Breeding industries are being consistently improved worldwide because of the increasing demand for beef, veal, pork, and poultry in developing countries (Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014). However, previous studies on pigs (McAuliffe et al. 2016; Reckmann et al. 2012), beef (Asem-Hiablie et al. 2019; Beauchemin et al. 2010; Bragaglio 2018), rab- bit (Cesari et al. 2018; Da et al. 2014; Zened et al. 2013), and poultry (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008; Boggia et al. 2010; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; M Da et al. 2014; Nunez et al. 2005; Rocchi et al. 2019; Schenck and Huizenga 2020; Silva et al. 2012; Williams et al. 2020) were mainly conducted in the farm stage, and only a few studies have been performed in the slaughtering stage. Analysis from a cradle-to-grave perspective to obtain comprehensive information on the whole industry chain (including breeding, slaughter, and waste material treatment) has rarely been performed (Bo et al. 2008; Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014; Josue Lopez-Andres et al. 2018; Skunca et al. 2018), and there is limited research on the rabbit breeding industry.

Cesari (Cesari et al. 2018) analyzed the environmental impact of the rabbit production system by using a life cycle approach from the cradle-to-slaughterhouse perspective. The results obtained for all scenarios confirmed that the most important hotspot with respect to the environmental impact of rabbit meat production is feed, which is consistent with the findings of other studies on different species. Previous studies on rabbits (Cesari et al. 2018; Zened et al. 2013) have shown that feed production is the main contributor to environmental impacts; for example, soybean meal has been associated with deforestation-related emissions. Recently, the number of studies on the rex rabbit breed- ing industry has increased. However, most of the studies on rex rabbits focused on the skin, diet, and genes. Tao (Tao 1994) compared the rex rabbit with the New Zealand white rabbit and found that the rex rabbit has a higher proportion of fine hairs and hair density than the New Zealand white rabbit, with characteristics such as shortness and homoge- neity. Guo-Sun (Guo-Shun et al. 2012) studied the effects

of Chinese herbal medicine additives on the meat, fur, and hide quality of rex rabbits. Other studies on the rex rabbit were mainly related to gene expression (Niu et al. 2019; Widiyastuti et al. 2014; Zhao et al. 2017), with only a few related to reproductive capacity (Pei et al. 2012). Therefore, there is limited information on the life cycle environmental impacts of the rex rabbit industry chain.

The circular economy (CE) is beneficial to the environ- ment, and it has been considered to be the best model for sustainable development (Cimen 2021). The traditional eco- nomic development model is based on a linear economy. However, a closed-loop CE improves the management of secondary materials and the waste supply chain to main- tain the value of products or materials (Salmenperä et al. 2021). The use of CE in agriculture has been a highlight in a worldwide context. CE alternatives can provide consist- ent benefits to agribusinesses, given the better management of raw materials and waste, production of cleaner energy, increase in energy efficiency, and cost avoidance (Barros et al. 2020). Global applications of CE have been reported in several areas, such as mushroom cultivation (Grimm and Wösten 2018), agrifood (Caruso et al. 2019; Pagotto and Halog 2016), bioenergy (Zabaniotou 2018), biogas (Kapoor et al. 2020), fertilizer application (Bekchanov and Mirzabaev 2018; Mosquera-Losada et al. 2019), and animal production (Dumont et al. 2012).

Recently, China introduced many policies and guide- lines to promote environmental protection and CE (Xu et al. 2021). The development of ecological circular agriculture has been highly encouraged by the Chinese government to recycle agricultural wastes, reduce mineral fertilizer input, and protect the environment (Chen et al. 2017). The con- cept of CE is also receiving more attention in the rex rabbit breeding industry. Some rex rabbit breeding enterprises have proactively attempted to establish a circular industry chain instead of the traditional industry chain, for example, reduc- ing chemical fertilizer consumption and recycling wastes by adding manure treatment and biogas generation stages. However, the addition of new stages requires materials and energy, which may cause new environmental problems. Therefore, it is necessary to compare the environmental impacts of the traditional and circular rex rabbit industry chains from a life cycle perspective.

The main aim of this study was to analyze the environ- mental impacts of the traditional rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 1) and circular rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 2) from a cradle-to-gate perspective by using the attribu- tional LCA method, and to determine whether the circular rex rabbit industry chain has better environmental feasi- bility and benefits. A transparent cradle-to-gate life cycle inventory of the two scenarios was established through field investigation of a rex rabbit enterprise in China. The envi- ronmental impacts of the two scenarios were assessed, and

the largest contributor to each impact category was found. A production pattern with better environmental performance was determined as the future practice direction by perform- ing a benefit analysis. This study provides a theoretical and practical reference for selecting a more sustainable breed- ing pattern and optimizing the industrial structure of the rex rabbit breeding and related industries, thus promoting sustainable development.

Recently, China introduced many policies and guide- lines to promote environmental protection and CE (Xu et al. 2021). The development of ecological circular agriculture has been highly encouraged by the Chinese government to recycle agricultural wastes, reduce mineral fertilizer input, and protect the environment (Chen et al. 2017). The con- cept of CE is also receiving more attention in the rex rabbit breeding industry. Some rex rabbit breeding enterprises have proactively attempted to establish a circular industry chain instead of the traditional industry chain, for example, reduc- ing chemical fertilizer consumption and recycling wastes by adding manure treatment and biogas generation stages. However, the addition of new stages requires materials and energy, which may cause new environmental problems. Therefore, it is necessary to compare the environmental impacts of the traditional and circular rex rabbit industry chains from a life cycle perspective.

The main aim of this study was to analyze the environ- mental impacts of the traditional rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 1) and circular rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 2) from a cradle-to-gate perspective by using the attribu- tional LCA method, and to determine whether the circular rex rabbit industry chain has better environmental feasi- bility and benefits. A transparent cradle-to-gate life cycle inventory of the two scenarios was established through field investigation of a rex rabbit enterprise in China. The envi- ronmental impacts of the two scenarios were assessed, and

the largest contributor to each impact category was found. A production pattern with better environmental performance was determined as the future practice direction by perform- ing a benefit analysis. This study provides a theoretical and practical reference for selecting a more sustainable breed- ing pattern and optimizing the industrial structure of the rex rabbit breeding and related industries, thus promoting sustainable development.
7: By 2075, the world human population is predicted to reach 9.2 billion (Alexandratos and Bruinsma 2012). Therefore, the agriculture sector needs to enhance its efficiency to feed the population (Crosson et al. 2011). However, con- sumers have shown an important evolution towards high- quality food produced in environment-friendly conditions (de Boer 2003; Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014; Iribarren et al. 2011; Sala et al. 2017).

Rabbits have extremely high economic and practical value, and rabbit meat has great nutritional value and dietetic properties; it is characterized by high protein content and low fat content (Cesari et al. 2018). Rabbit fur has characteristics such as shortness, fitness, density, smoothness, and beauty (Tao 1994). The rex rabbit yields both meat and fur; it is suitable for breeding in China, as its feeding range covers many provinces. The rex rabbit breeding industry plays an important role in improving income and driving local development (Yang and Hong- Jun 2010).

With the development of the rex rabbit industry and increasing quantity and quality requirements of domestic and international markets for rex rabbit products, China's rex rabbit breeding industry has developed from traditional feeding to large-scale, industrialized, specialized, and environment-friendly breeding and processing. Nowadays, conventional rex rabbit production has three main stages: breeding, slaughtering, and manure treatment. Rex rab- bits are bred and raised until they gain the desired weight. Then, they are sent to the slaughterhouse to collect their meat and skin. Manure is considered a byproduct; rab- bit manure and urine are high-quality organic fertilizers. An adult rabbit can yield more than 100 kg of fertilizer per year, and 10 adult rabbits provide fertilizer equivalent to that yielded by a pig (Istikomah 2011). The nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium contents in rabbit manure are higher than those in the manure of other domestic animals. Rabbit manure contains about 7.7 times, 3.8 times, and 3.3 times more nitrogen than pig, sheep, and chicken manure, respectively (Istikomah 2011). Every 100 kg of rabbit manure is equivalent to 10.85 kg of ammonium sulfate, 10.85 kg of ammonium superphosphate, 10.90 kg of cal- cium superphosphate, and 1.79 kg of potassium superacid (Wu et al. 2010).

However, rapid expansion of the rex rabbit industry has led to a disordered increase in the number of farms and extensive breeding patterns, resulting in poor quality (Pei et al. 2012). Each link of the rex rabbit industry chain also requires large amounts of energy and raw materials, lead- ing to environmental impacts (López-Andrés et al. 2018). The breeding of rex rabbits may cause serious wastage
of valuable resources and environmental pollution (Xiong and Zhu 2007). Therefore, it is necessary to assess the environmental impacts of the rex rabbit breeding industry.

Different methods have been developed to evaluate the environmental impacts of products or production systems. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is one of the most common methods used to analyze environmental impacts (Nwe et al. 2010; Roy et al. 2009). It is a standardized and holistic meth- odology that allows for the identification of the environmen- tal consequences of the life cycle of a product by evaluating potential environmental impacts over the entire life cycle production chain. Many researchers have used LCA to evalu- ate a wide range of breeding industries (Roy et al. 2009).

Breeding industries are being consistently improved worldwide because of the increasing demand for beef, veal, pork, and poultry in developing countries (Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014). However, previous studies on pigs (McAuliffe et al. 2016; Reckmann et al. 2012), beef (Asem-Hiablie et al. 2019; Beauchemin et al. 2010; Bragaglio 2018), rab- bit (Cesari et al. 2018; Da et al. 2014; Zened et al. 2013), and poultry (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008; Boggia et al. 2010; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; M Da et al. 2014; Nunez et al. 2005; Rocchi et al. 2019; Schenck and Huizenga 2020; Silva et al. 2012; Williams et al. 2020) were mainly conducted in the farm stage, and only a few studies have been performed in the slaughtering stage. Analysis from a cradle-to-grave perspective to obtain comprehensive information on the whole industry chain (including breeding, slaughter, and waste material treatment) has rarely been performed (Bo et al. 2008; Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014; Josue Lopez-Andres et al. 2018; Skunca et al. 2018), and there is limited research on the rabbit breeding industry.

Cesari (Cesari et al. 2018) analyzed the environmental impact of the rabbit production system by using a life cycle approach from the cradle-to-slaughterhouse perspective. The results obtained for all scenarios confirmed that the most important hotspot with respect to the environmental impact of rabbit meat production is feed, which is consistent with the findings of other studies on different species. Previous studies on rabbits (Cesari et al. 2018; Zened et al. 2013) have shown that feed production is the main contributor to environmental impacts; for example, soybean meal has been associated with deforestation-related emissions. Recently, the number of studies on the rex rabbit breed- ing industry has increased. However, most of the studies on rex rabbits focused on the skin, diet, and genes. Tao (Tao 1994) compared the rex rabbit with the New Zealand white rabbit and found that the rex rabbit has a higher proportion of fine hairs and hair density than the New Zealand white rabbit, with characteristics such as shortness and homoge- neity. Guo-Sun (Guo-Shun et al. 2012) studied the effects

of Chinese herbal medicine additives on the meat, fur, and hide quality of rex rabbits. Other studies on the rex rabbit were mainly related to gene expression (Niu et al. 2019; Widiyastuti et al. 2014; Zhao et al. 2017), with only a few related to reproductive capacity (Pei et al. 2012). Therefore, there is limited information on the life cycle environmental impacts of the rex rabbit industry chain.

The circular economy (CE) is beneficial to the environ- ment, and it has been considered to be the best model for sustainable development (Cimen 2021). The traditional eco- nomic development model is based on a linear economy. However, a closed-loop CE improves the management of secondary materials and the waste supply chain to main- tain the value of products or materials (Salmenperä et al. 2021). The use of CE in agriculture has been a highlight in a worldwide context. CE alternatives can provide consist- ent benefits to agribusinesses, given the better management of raw materials and waste, production of cleaner energy, increase in energy efficiency, and cost avoidance (Barros et al. 2020). Global applications of CE have been reported in several areas, such as mushroom cultivation (Grimm and Wösten 2018), agrifood (Caruso et al. 2019; Pagotto and Halog 2016), bioenergy (Zabaniotou 2018), biogas (Kapoor et al. 2020), fertilizer application (Bekchanov and Mirzabaev 2018; Mosquera-Losada et al. 2019), and animal production (Dumont et al. 2012).

The development of ecological circular agriculture has been highly encouraged by the Chinese government to recycle agricultural wastes, reduce mineral fertilizer input, and protect the environment (Chen et al. 2017). The con- cept of CE is also receiving more attention in the rex rabbit breeding industry. Some rex rabbit breeding enterprises have proactively attempted to establish a circular industry chain instead of the traditional industry chain, for example, reduc- ing chemical fertilizer consumption and recycling wastes by adding manure treatment and biogas generation stages. However, the addition of new stages requires materials and energy, which may cause new environmental problems. Therefore, it is necessary to compare the environmental impacts of the traditional and circular rex rabbit industry chains from a life cycle perspective.

The main aim of this study was to analyze the environ- mental impacts of the traditional rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 1) and circular rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 2) from a cradle-to-gate perspective by using the attribu- tional LCA method, and to determine whether the circular rex rabbit industry chain has better environmental feasi- bility and benefits. A transparent cradle-to-gate life cycle inventory of the two scenarios was established through field investigation of a rex rabbit enterprise in China. The envi- ronmental impacts of the two scenarios were assessed, and

the largest contributor to each impact category was found. A production pattern with better environmental performance was determined as the future practice direction by perform- ing a benefit analysis. This study provides a theoretical and practical reference for selecting a more sustainable breed- ing pattern and optimizing the industrial structure of the rex rabbit breeding and related industries, thus promoting sustainable development.

Guo-Sun (Guo-Shun et al. 2012) studied the effects

of Chinese herbal medicine additives on the meat, fur, and hide quality of rex rabbits. Other studies on the rex rabbit were mainly related to gene expression (Niu et al. 2019; Widiyastuti et al. 2014; Zhao et al. 2017), with only a few related to reproductive capacity (Pei et al. 2012). Therefore, there is limited information on the life cycle environmental impacts of the rex rabbit industry chain.

The circular economy (CE) is beneficial to the environ- ment, and it has been considered to be the best model for sustainable development (Cimen 2021). The traditional eco- nomic development model is based on a linear economy. However, a closed-loop CE improves the management of secondary materials and the waste supply chain to main- tain the value of products or materials (Salmenperä et al. 2021). The use of CE in agriculture has been a highlight in a worldwide context. CE alternatives can provide consist- ent benefits to agribusinesses, given the better management of raw materials and waste, production of cleaner energy, increase in energy efficiency, and cost avoidance (Barros et al. 2020). Global applications of CE have been reported in several areas, such as mushroom cultivation (Grimm and Wösten 2018), agrifood (Caruso et al. 2019; Pagotto and Halog 2016), bioenergy (Zabaniotou 2018), biogas (Kapoor et al. 2020), fertilizer application (Bekchanov and Mirzabaev 2018; Mosquera-Losada et al. 2019), and animal production (Dumont et al. 2012).

Recently, China introduced many policies and guide- lines to promote environmental protection and CE (Xu et al. 2021). The development of ecological circular agriculture has been highly encouraged by the Chinese government to recycle agricultural wastes, reduce mineral fertilizer input, and protect the environment (Chen et al. 2017). The con- cept of CE is also receiving more attention in the rex rabbit breeding industry. Some rex rabbit breeding enterprises have proactively attempted to establish a circular industry chain instead of the traditional industry chain, for example, reduc- ing chemical fertilizer consumption and recycling wastes by adding manure treatment and biogas generation stages. However, the addition of new stages requires materials and energy, which may cause new environmental problems. Therefore, it is necessary to compare the environmental impacts of the traditional and circular rex rabbit industry chains from a life cycle perspective.

The main aim of this study was to analyze the environ- mental impacts of the traditional rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 1) and circular rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 2) from a cradle-to-gate perspective by using the attribu- tional LCA method, and to determine whether the circular rex rabbit industry chain has better environmental feasi- bility and benefits. A transparent cradle-to-gate life cycle inventory of the two scenarios was established through field investigation of a rex rabbit enterprise in China. The envi- ronmental impacts of the two scenarios were assessed, and

the largest contributor to each impact category was found. A production pattern with better environmental performance was determined as the future practice direction by perform- ing a benefit analysis. This study provides a theoretical and practical reference for selecting a more sustainable breed- ing pattern and optimizing the industrial structure of the rex rabbit breeding and related industries, thus promoting sustainable development.
11: By 2075, the world human population is predicted to reach 9.2 billion (Alexandratos and Bruinsma 2012). Therefore, the agriculture sector needs to enhance its efficiency to feed the population (Crosson et al. 2011). However, con- sumers have shown an important evolution towards high- quality food produced in environment-friendly conditions (de Boer 2003; Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014; Iribarren et al. 2011; Sala et al. 2017).

Rabbits have extremely high economic and practical value, and rabbit meat has great nutritional value and dietetic properties; it is characterized by high protein content and low fat content (Cesari et al. 2018). Rabbit fur has characteristics such as shortness, fitness, density, smoothness, and beauty (Tao 1994). The rex rabbit yields both meat and fur; it is suitable for breeding in China, as its feeding range covers many provinces. The rex rabbit breeding industry plays an important role in improving income and driving local development (Yang and Hong- Jun 2010).

With the development of the rex rabbit industry and increasing quantity and quality requirements of domestic and international markets for rex rabbit products, China's rex rabbit breeding industry has developed from traditional feeding to large-scale, industrialized, specialized, and environment-friendly breeding and processing. Nowadays, conventional rex rabbit production has three main stages: breeding, slaughtering, and manure treatment. Rex rab- bits are bred and raised until they gain the desired weight. Then, they are sent to the slaughterhouse to collect their meat and skin. Manure is considered a byproduct; rab- bit manure and urine are high-quality organic fertilizers. An adult rabbit can yield more than 100 kg of fertilizer per year, and 10 adult rabbits provide fertilizer equivalent to that yielded by a pig (Istikomah 2011). The nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium contents in rabbit manure are higher than those in the manure of other domestic animals. Rabbit manure contains about 7.7 times, 3.8 times, and 3.3 times more nitrogen than pig, sheep, and chicken manure, respectively (Istikomah 2011). Every 100 kg of rabbit manure is equivalent to 10.85 kg of ammonium sulfate, 10.85 kg of ammonium superphosphate, 10.90 kg of cal- cium superphosphate, and 1.79 kg of potassium superacid (Wu et al. 2010).

However, rapid expansion of the rex rabbit industry has led to a disordered increase in the number of farms and extensive breeding patterns, resulting in poor quality (Pei et al. 2012). Each link of the rex rabbit industry chain also requires large amounts of energy and raw materials, lead- ing to environmental impacts (López-Andrés et al. 2018). The breeding of rex rabbits may cause serious wastage
of valuable resources and environmental pollution (Xiong and Zhu 2007). Therefore, it is necessary to assess the environmental impacts of the rex rabbit breeding industry.

Different methods have been developed to evaluate the environmental impacts of products or production systems. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is one of the most common methods used to analyze environmental impacts (Nwe et al. 2010; Roy et al. 2009). It is a standardized and holistic meth- odology that allows for the identification of the environmen- tal consequences of the life cycle of a product by evaluating potential environmental impacts over the entire life cycle production chain. Many researchers have used LCA to evalu- ate a wide range of breeding industries (Roy et al. 2009).

Breeding industries are being consistently improved worldwide because of the increasing demand for beef, veal, pork, and poultry in developing countries (Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014). However, previous studies on pigs (McAuliffe et al. 2016; Reckmann et al. 2012), beef (Asem-Hiablie et al. 2019; Beauchemin et al. 2010; Bragaglio 2018), rab- bit (Cesari et al. 2018; Da et al. 2014; Zened et al. 2013), and poultry (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008; Boggia et al. 2010; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; M Da et al. 2014; Nunez et al. 2005; Rocchi et al. 2019; Schenck and Huizenga 2020; Silva et al. 2012; Williams et al. 2020) were mainly conducted in the farm stage, and only a few studies have been performed in the slaughtering stage. Analysis from a cradle-to-grave perspective to obtain comprehensive information on the whole industry chain (including breeding, slaughter, and waste material treatment) has rarely been performed (Bo et al. 2008; Gonzalez-Garcia et al. 2014; Josue Lopez-Andres et al. 2018; Skunca et al. 2018), and there is limited research on the rabbit breeding industry.

Cesari (Cesari et al. 2018) analyzed the environmental impact of the rabbit production system by using a life cycle approach from the cradle-to-slaughterhouse perspective. The results obtained for all scenarios confirmed that the most important hotspot with respect to the environmental impact of rabbit meat production is feed, which is consistent with the findings of other studies on different species. Previous studies on rabbits (Cesari et al. 2018; Zened et al. 2013) have shown that feed production is the main contributor to environmental impacts; for example, soybean meal has been associated with deforestation-related emissions. Recently, the number of studies on the rex rabbit breed- ing industry has increased. However, most of the studies on rex rabbits focused on the skin, diet, and genes. Tao (Tao 1994) compared the rex rabbit with the New Zealand white rabbit and found that the rex rabbit has a higher proportion of fine hairs and hair density than the New Zealand white rabbit, with characteristics such as shortness and homoge- neity. Guo-Sun (Guo-Shun et al. 2012) studied the effects

of Chinese herbal medicine additives on the meat, fur, and hide quality of rex rabbits. Other studies on the rex rabbit were mainly related to gene expression (Niu et al. 2019; Widiyastuti et al. 2014; Zhao et al. 2017), with only a few related to reproductive capacity (Pei et al. 2012). Therefore, there is limited information on the life cycle environmental impacts of the rex rabbit industry chain.

The circular economy (CE) is beneficial to the environ- ment, and it has been considered to be the best model for sustainable development (Cimen 2021). The traditional eco- nomic development model is based on a linear economy. However, a closed-loop CE improves the management of secondary materials and the waste supply chain to main- tain the value of products or materials (Salmenperä et al. 2021). The use of CE in agriculture has been a highlight in a worldwide context. CE alternatives can provide consist- ent benefits to agribusinesses, given the better management of raw materials and waste, production of cleaner energy, increase in energy efficiency, and cost avoidance (Barros et al. 2020). Global applications of CE have been reported in several areas, such as mushroom cultivation (Grimm and Wösten 2018), agrifood (Caruso et al. 2019; Pagotto and Halog 2016), bioenergy (Zabaniotou 2018), biogas (Kapoor et al. 2020), fertilizer application (Bekchanov and Mirzabaev 2018; Mosquera-Losada et al. 2019), and animal production (Dumont et al. 2012).

Recently, China introduced many policies and guide- lines to promote environmental protection and CE (Xu et al. 2021). The development of ecological circular agriculture has been highly encouraged by the Chinese government to recycle agricultural wastes, reduce mineral fertilizer input, and protect the environment (Chen et al. 2017). The con- cept of CE is also receiving more attention in the rex rabbit breeding industry. Some rex rabbit breeding enterprises have proactively attempted to establish a circular industry chain instead of the traditional industry chain, for example, reduc- ing chemical fertilizer consumption and recycling wastes by adding manure treatment and biogas generation stages. However, the addition of new stages requires materials and energy, which may cause new environmental problems. Therefore, it is necessary to compare the environmental impacts of the traditional and circular rex rabbit industry chains from a life cycle perspective.

The main aim of this study was to analyze the environ- mental impacts of the traditional rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 1) and circular rex rabbit industry chain (scenario 2) from a cradle-to-gate perspective by using the attribu- tional LCA method, and to determine whether the circular rex rabbit industry chain has better environmental feasi- bility and benefits. A transparent cradle-to-gate life cycle inventory of the two scenarios was established through field investigation of a rex rabbit enterprise in China. The envi- ronmental impacts of the two scenarios were assessed, and

the largest contributor to each impact category was found. A production pattern with better environmental performance was determined as the future practice direction by perform- ing a benefit analysis. This study provides a theoretical and practical reference for selecting a more sustainable breed- ing pattern and optimizing the industrial structure of the rex rabbit breeding and related industries, thus promoting sustainable development.

13: The environmental profiles were established and com- parative analyses were performed using LCA methodol- ogy, standardized by ISO 14040 (ISO 2006a) and ISO 14044 (ISO 2006b). According to the standards, four main steps were implemented: (1) goal and scope defini- tion, (2) inventory analysis, (3) impact assessment, and (4) result interpretation. We chose a typical rex rabbit breed- ing enterprise in China to conduct the attributional LCA. The case background was introduced. Then, the goal and scope of our study were defined, and a transparent cradle- to-gate life cycle inventory (LCI) of the two scenarios was provided through field investigation of the case enterprise. SimaPro8.0.4 software was used to conduct the impact assessment. The results have been interpreted in the Results and Discussion section. Specific methodological aspects are described in the following subsections.
14: The case enterprise is located at the eastern end of the Kubuqi Desert, China. It covers an area of 53 km2 with 10,000 rex rabbit breeding households, annual breeding capacity of 25 million rex rabbits, and average output of 13 million rex rabbits in 2016, 2017, and 2018. Recently, the enterprise has been committed to CE practices. On the basis of the traditional rex rabbit breeding industry chain, i.e., feed cultivation and processing, rex rabbit breeding, rex rabbit slaughtering, and product sale, the enterprise has formed a relatively complete circular industry chain by reducing chemical fertilizer and pesticide consumption and recycling waste through the addition of manure treatment and biogas generation stages. The original industry chain has also been extended by introducing a fur garment processing stage. However, during the field investigations, the feed cultivation and processing, rex rabbit breeding base, and slaughtering plant were working; the manure treatment and biogas power generation plant was operating on a trial basis; and the gar- ment processing stage was suspended because of market impact. Therefore, the fur garment processing stage was not considered in this study.
15: The goal of the LCA was to compare the environmental impacts of the traditional rex rabbit industry chain and circular rex rabbit industry chain and verify whether the circular rex rabbit industry chain has better environmental benefits. The research scope involved all stages of the tradi- tional industry chain and circular industry chain built within the case enterprise from a cradle-to-gate perspective. The environment-related auxiliary materials and energy supplies from the cradle were also contained, for example, electricity production, water supply, fertilizer production, and pesticide production. Activities outside the gate of the case enterprise were not considered in this study.
16: Scenario 1: The traditional rex rabbit industry chain (S1) includes the feed cultivation, feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, and rex rabbit slaughtering stages. In this sce- nario, feedstuffs like maize, wheat, and soybean were culti- vated with chemical fertilizers and wastes such as rex rab- bit manure and offal were directly transported outside the system for sale or outsourcing treatment, without reusing or recycling within the case enterprise. Because the research scope was from cradle to gate, we did not consider the waste treatment in S1.

Scenario 2: The circular rex rabbit industry chain (S2) includes the feed cultivation, feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, rex rabbit slaughtering, manure treatment, and biogas power generation stages. According to the principles of CE, the manure produced during the rex rabbit breeding stage was composted anaerobically, and the biogas produced was recycled to generate electricity and traced back to the rex rabbit breeding and feed processing stages. The biogas slurry and slag were used as organic fertilizers and recycled back to the feed cultivation stage to reduce chemical ferti- lizer and pesticide consumption. In this scenario, most of the wastes were reused or recycled, and the material and energy consumption from outside the system were significantly reduced. This almost formed a closed-loop cycle process of "resources-products-reborn resources", SO it was called the circular industry chain.
17: Scenario 1: The traditional rex rabbit industry chain (S1) includes the feed cultivation, feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, and rex rabbit slaughtering stages. In this sce- nario, feedstuffs like maize, wheat, and soybean were culti- vated with chemical fertilizers and wastes such as rex rab- bit manure and offal were directly transported outside the system for sale or outsourcing treatment, without reusing or recycling within the case enterprise. Because the research scope was from cradle to gate, we did not consider the waste treatment in S1.

Scenario 2: The circular rex rabbit industry chain (S2) includes the feed cultivation, feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, rex rabbit slaughtering, manure treatment, and biogas power generation stages. According to the principles of CE, the manure produced during the rex rabbit breeding stage was composted anaerobically, and the biogas produced was recycled to generate electricity and traced back to the rex rabbit breeding and feed processing stages. The biogas slurry and slag were used as organic fertilizers and recycled back to the feed cultivation stage to reduce chemical ferti- lizer and pesticide consumption. In this scenario, most of the wastes were reused or recycled, and the material and energy consumption from outside the system were significantly reduced. This almost formed a closed-loop cycle process of "resources-products-reborn resources", SO it was called the circular industry chain.
18: We set the system boundary as "from cradle to gate" and determined the system boundaries for the two scenarios (Fig. 1).
19: According to data from the enterprise, a rex rabbit's fur, hair density, and color are the best when it reaches the standard weight of 2.6 kg after 140 days of breeding. Therefore, the functional unit of this study was an average rex rabbit dur- ing 140 days of breeding. The reference flows for each stage were converted in terms of the functional unit. Accordingly, the reference flow of feed cultivation and feed processing stages was 39 kg feed needed for 140 days of breeding, and the reference flow of rabbit breeding and slaughtering stages was an average rex rabbit.
20: In the feed cultivation stage, three types of feed (maize, soy- bean, and alfalfa-grass) were cultivated by the enterprise, and the other feeds (wheat bran, sunflower seed meal, and rice straw) were purchased from outside the enterprise. Therefore, we assumed the following for the feed cultiva- tion stage: the biogas slurry and slag from the manure treat- ment stage were recycled as organic fertilizers to replace all the chemical fertilizers and insecticides used for pro- ducing maize, soybean, and alfalfa-grass, as biogas slurry and slag can be used as organic fertilizers and insecticides (Gupta and Sharma 2002; Jothi et al. 2003; Li 1994; Lu et al. 2012; Sheets et al. 2015; Tan et al. 2016). The production processes of the remaining feeds were the same in the two scenarios.

In the manure treatment and biogas power generation stages, 36.2 kg manure was produced by one rabbit during 140 days of breeding and 36.2 kg of manure yielded 4.27 kWh of electricity, which was not sufficient for electricity consumption in all other stages of the industry chain. There- fore, we assumed the following for the recycling of elec- tricity produced in the biogas power generation stage: the electricity was first recycled to the rex rabbit breeding stage, which is closest to the manure treatment and biogas power generation stages, that is, 0.836 kWh outsourced power orig- inally consumed by S1 in the breeding stage was replaced, and the remaining 3.343 kWh was recycled to the feed pro- cessing stage to replace part of the outsourced electricity.

The transport processes in the feed cultivation, feed pro- cessing, and rex rabbit breeding stages were not considered because of the short distances within the enterprise. We only considered the transport process from the rex rabbit breeding stage to the slaughtering stage, with a transport distance of 20 km by truck; this was converted to 0.0104 tkm, according to the functional unit. The dataset "transport, freight, lorry 7.5-16 metric ton, EURO6 {GLO}" from the Ecoinvent 3.0 database was used for the transport process in this study. The transport of outsourced feeds or transport in other stages were not considered in our study because of the lack of data.

23: In the feed cultivation stage, three types of feed (maize, soy- bean, and alfalfa-grass) were cultivated by the enterprise, and the other feeds (wheat bran, sunflower seed meal, and rice straw) were purchased from outside the enterprise. Therefore, we assumed the following for the feed cultiva- tion stage: the biogas slurry and slag from the manure treat- ment stage were recycled as organic fertilizers to replace all the chemical fertilizers and insecticides used for pro- ducing maize, soybean, and alfalfa-grass, as biogas slurry and slag can be used as organic fertilizers and insecticides (Gupta and Sharma 2002; Jothi et al. 2003; Li 1994; Lu et al. 2012; Sheets et al. 2015; Tan et al. 2016). The production processes of the remaining feeds were the same in the two scenarios.

In the manure treatment and biogas power generation stages, 36.2 kg manure was produced by one rabbit during 140 days of breeding and 36.2 kg of manure yielded 4.27 kWh of electricity, which was not sufficient for electricity consumption in all other stages of the industry chain. There- fore, we assumed the following for the recycling of elec- tricity produced in the biogas power generation stage: the electricity was first recycled to the rex rabbit breeding stage, which is closest to the manure treatment and biogas power generation stages, that is, 0.836 kWh outsourced power orig- inally consumed by S1 in the breeding stage was replaced, and the remaining 3.343 kWh was recycled to the feed pro- cessing stage to replace part of the outsourced electricity.

The transport processes in the feed cultivation, feed pro- cessing, and rex rabbit breeding stages were not considered because of the short distances within the enterprise. We only considered the transport process from the rex rabbit breeding stage to the slaughtering stage, with a transport distance of 20 km by truck; this was converted to 0.0104 tkm, according to the functional unit. The dataset "transport, freight, lorry 7.5-16 metric ton, EURO6 {GLO}" from the Ecoinvent 3.0 database was used for the transport process in this study. The transport of outsourced feeds or transport in other stages were not considered in our study because of the lack of data.

27: <header>2.3.1 LCI of the feed cultivation stage </header>

Because the field investigations were performed in winter, we did not obtain the data for the feed cultivation stage; therefore, we used production data for maize, soybean, and alfalfa-grass from the Ecoinvent 3.0 database as the LCI of the feed cultivation stage for S1 and replaced all the chemi- cal fertilizers and insecticides with biogas slurry and slag as the LCI of the feed cultivation stage for S2. The data for the production of other feeds were also obtained from the Ecoinvent 3.0 database. The specific LCI for this stage is provided in Table S1-6 in the supplementary material.
28: <header>2.3.2 LCI of the feed processing stage </header>

During the 140 days of rex rabbit breeding, a total of 39 kg of feed was consumed; therefore, the reference flow of the feed processing stage was 39 kg of feed. Maize grain, maize flour, maize bran, maize germ, soybean meal, alfalfa-grass, wheat bran, sunflower seed meal, and rice straw were used as raw materials for the feed processing. The difference between S1 and S2 was that 3.434 kWh external electric power supply in S1 was replaced with electricity produced in the biogas power generation stage in S2. The specific LCI of this stage is shown in Table 1 (also Table S7 in the sup- plementary material).
29: <header>2.3.3 LCI of the rex rabbit breeding stage </header>

In the rex rabbit breeding stage of S1, water, electricity, and finished feed were mainly consumed. In S2, the electric power was obtained from the biogas power generation stage, SO the electricity consumption was calculated as zero in this stage and no external electricity was consumed. The rex rab- bit was bred until it weighed 2.6 kg in both scenarios. The specific LCI of this stage is shown in Tables 2 and 3 (also Tables S8 and S9 in the supplementary material).
30: <header>2.3.4 LCI of the rex rabbit slaughtering stage </header>

The rex rabbit slaughtering stage was an assembly-line pro- cess, in which electricity and water were mainly consumed, as well as cartons for packaging. The case enterprise slaugh- tered 300,000 rex rabbits per year on average, with meat and fur products as the outputs. Most of the meat products were sold as a whole, including offal and blood, and only a very small number of purchasers required gutting (according to the field investigation and communication with staff in the enterprise, the number was < 1%). Because the enterprise also raises other animals such as ermines and dogs, the rex rabbit offal produced in the slaughtering stage was provided as feed to these animals. Because of the uncertainty of the output and limited data, the rex rabbit offal treatment was not considered in our study. The specific LCI of this stage is shown in Table 4 (also Table S10 in the supplementary material).

investigations, the relevant data were incomplete. We obtained only the input data for materials and energy and the output data for products and byproducts. The data for pollutant emissions were absent, SO data were obtained from the literature. In the manure treatment stage, biogas production from manure was 0.06 m³-kg-1 (Li et al. 2018); therefore, 36.2 kg of manure produced 2.17 m³ of biogas. The biogas generated 1.97 kWh of electricity per cubic meter (Ciotola et al. 2011) and 4.27 kWh of electric- ity per functional unit, and CO2 emission from biogas combustion was calculated using a previously described method (Ciotola et al. 2011). According to the Agricul- tural Trade Standards of the People's Republic of China (NY/T1704-2009), H2S content in biogas cannot be more than 250 mg.m-3 when the methane content is 50%-60%; when the biogas was purified by anaerobic fermentation, SO2 emission was 0.542 g per functional unit in the biogas power generation stage (Ciotola et al. 2011). The specific LCI of the manure treatment and biogas power generation stages is shown in Tables 5 and 6 (also Tables S11 and S12 in the supplementary material).

34: The LCA software SimaPro8.0.4 was used to evaluate the environmental impacts of the two scenarios. The CML 2001- Nov.2010 method, a well-established and widely used mid- point methodology, was used for the characterization and normalization of the environmental impacts (Andrade et al. 2021; Muazu et al. 2021; Soleimani and Gilbert 2021). In this method, the following 11 impact categories were consid- ered: abiotic depletion potential (ADP, kg Sb eq), acidification potential (AP, kg SO2 eq), eutrophication potential (EP, kg PO43 eq), freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity potential (FAETP, kg 1,4-DCB eq), global warming potential (GWP, kg CO2 eq), land competition potential (LCP, yr), human toxic- ity potential (HTP, kg 1,4-DCB eq), ozone depletion poten- tial (ODP, kg CFC-11 eq), ionizing radiation potential (IRP, DALYs), photochemical ozone creation potential (POCP, kg C2H4 eq), and terrestric ecotoxicity potential (TETP, kg 1,4- DCB eq). To ensure integrity, we selected all categories to further analyze and discuss the characterization and normali- zation results for the two scenarios.

Normalization, as defined in the ISO 14044 standard (ISO 2006b), is the magnitude of the category indictor results relative to some reference information. The aim of normali- zation is to better understand the relative magnitude for each indicator result of the product system under study. Normali- zation transforms an impact indicator result by dividing it by a selected reference value. In our study, the reference value was the total inputs and outputs for the world, 1995 (Guinée et al. 2002; Huijbregts et al. 2003), as described in the CML 2001-Nov.2010 method. The normalization factors are provided in the supplementary material.

Normalization, as defined in the ISO 14044 standard (ISO 2006b), is the magnitude of the category indictor results relative to some reference information. The aim of normali- zation is to better understand the relative magnitude for each indicator result of the product system under study. Normali- zation transforms an impact indicator result by dividing it by a selected reference value. In our study, the reference value was the total inputs and outputs for the world, 1995 (Guinée et al. 2002; Huijbregts et al. 2003), as described in the CML 2001-Nov.2010 method. The normalization factors are provided in the supplementary material.
38: The specific characterization and normalization results of the two scenarios are provided in the supplementary material. Table 7 lists the total values of the environmental impacts for both scenarios. Figure 2 presents a comparison of relative contributions (in %) to each impact category from life cycle stages involved in the two scenarios on the basis of the char- acterization results. In both scenarios, the feed cultivation stage contributed most to the various environmental impact categories, followed by the feed processing stage. Of the 11 environmental impact categories, GWP, AP, EP, HTP, IRP, POCP, ODP, and LCP were mainly caused by the supply of electricity for the feed cultivation stage; TETP and FAETP were caused by the supply of electricity and fertilizers and pesticides used in the feed cultivation stage. ADP depended on the environmental effects of fossil fuel consumption as well as electricity supply. The feed processing stage also had obvious impacts on ADP, AP, GWP, HTP, and POCP. Elec- tric energy consumption was the main source of environment

Fig. Comparison of relative contributions (in %) to each impact category from life cycle stages involved in the two scenarios
impact in the feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, and rex rabbit slaughtering stages in each impact category.

In S2, manure treatment mainly contributed to AP, POCP, HTP, ADP, GWP, and EP. Addition of the biogas power generation stage resulted in negative values in each impact category, indicating environmental benefits. The electricity generated in the biogas power generation stage was traced back to the feed processing and rex rabbit breeding stages, which reduced the consumption of external electricity supply and environmental impacts. Therefore, even if S2 increases the industrial structure, the overall environment impacts of S2 are still lower than those of S1.

Figure 3 shows comparison of normalization results for the two scenarios. For S1, TETP was the most significant impact category, followed by FAETP, EP, AP, ADP, GWP, LCP, POCP, HTP, IRP, and ODP. TETP was mainly affected by the consumption of pesticides and chemical fertilizers during the cultivation of feed. While for S2, AP was the most significant impact category, followed by EP, ADP, TETP, FAETP, GWP, LCP, POCP, HTP, IRP, and ODP. In S2, TETP and FAETP were reduced by > 90%, and GWP, HTP, EP, ODP, ADP, and IRP were decreased by 7.2%-31.8%, compared with those in S1. However, AP increased by 119.6% in S2, mainly due to the generation of gases such as SO2 and NO during the manure treatment stage. LCP and POCP hardly changed between the scenarios. Because the value of ODP was too low, it was not analyzed further in our study.
Figure 4 shows the degree of change in S2 when com- pared with S1 with respect to the same life cycle stages and total value. On the basis of the total value, TETP showed

the greatest decrease, and AP, the greatest increase. The decrease of TETP and FAETP in S2 was mainly from the feed cultivation and rex rabbit breeding stages, accounting for > 50%. Because the electric energy generated by biogas was given priority in the rex rabbit breeding and feed pro- cessing stages, GWP, HTP, EP, ODP and IRP decreased to the greatest extent in these two stages. The environmen- tal impact of the rex rabbit breeding stage was zero in S2, mainly because the electricity was recycled in breeding stage (which is closest to the manure treatment and biogas power generation stages) and the outsourced power originally con- sumed in S1 in the breeding stage was replaced. The produc- tion of biogas slurry and slag in the manure treatment stage resulted in a decrease in the use of external fertilizers and insecticides in the feed cultivation stage. The ADP in S2 was reduced by 13.5% when compared with S1, with 68.1% and 85.5% contribution at the feed cultivation stage in S1 and S2, respectively. This was mainly attributable to the envi- ronmental impacts of oil and gas energy consumed in the feed cultivation stage and indirect consumption of electrical energy. Electrical energy supply mainly contributed to ADP in the other stages. However, an obvious increase in AP and POCP was observed in the manure treatment stage, mainly because of the simultaneous production of SO2, NO, and other gases from the biogas, biogas slurry, and slag produced during the manure treatment stage.

Because of the lack of relevant literature on the rex rabbit, our results were compared with the literature on LCA of poultry. As reported by several studies (Asem- Hiablie et al. 2019; McAuliffe et al. 2016; Sala et al. 2017; Skunca et al. 2018), feed cultivation is responsible
Fig. Comparison of normalization results for the two scenarios

to the greatest extent for the environmental impacts asso- ciated with farm-related activities. In our study, the feed cultivation stage was again responsible for the highest contributions in all impact categories because of the envi- ronmental impacts of various crop cultivations. These results are consistent with those of previous studies on poultry (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; Pelletier 2008; Silva et al. 2012; Williams et al. 2020). The contribu- tion of feed production to the environmental impacts of a chicken farm was especially high, ranging from 23% (in AP) to 79% (in TETP) (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008). Generally, in studies that include the slaugh- tering stage (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; Silva et al. 2012), the contri- bution of this stage to the environmental impacts is mini- mal. However, special attention must be paid to electricity
consumption in the slaughterhouse, as it is particularly high for electric shock production and refrigeration. The following is a detailed analysis of some important indicators.

Because of the lack of relevant literature on the rex rabbit, our results were compared with the literature on LCA of poultry. As reported by several studies (Asem- Hiablie et al. 2019; McAuliffe et al. 2016; Sala et al. 2017; Skunca et al. 2018), feed cultivation is responsible
Fig. Comparison of normalization results for the two scenarios

to the greatest extent for the environmental impacts asso- ciated with farm-related activities. In our study, the feed cultivation stage was again responsible for the highest contributions in all impact categories because of the envi- ronmental impacts of various crop cultivations. These results are consistent with those of previous studies on poultry (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; Pelletier 2008; Silva et al. 2012; Williams et al. 2020). The contribu- tion of feed production to the environmental impacts of a chicken farm was especially high, ranging from 23% (in AP) to 79% (in TETP) (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008). Generally, in studies that include the slaugh- tering stage (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; Silva et al. 2012), the contri- bution of this stage to the environmental impacts is mini- mal. However, special attention must be paid to electricity
consumption in the slaughterhouse, as it is particularly high for electric shock production and refrigeration. The following is a detailed analysis of some important indicators.

Table 7 lists the total values of the environmental impacts for both scenarios. Figure 2 presents a comparison of relative contributions (in %) to each impact category from life cycle stages involved in the two scenarios on the basis of the char- acterization results. In both scenarios, the feed cultivation stage contributed most to the various environmental impact categories, followed by the feed processing stage. Of the 11 environmental impact categories, GWP, AP, EP, HTP, IRP, POCP, ODP, and LCP were mainly caused by the supply of electricity for the feed cultivation stage; TETP and FAETP were caused by the supply of electricity and fertilizers and pesticides used in the feed cultivation stage. ADP depended on the environmental effects of fossil fuel consumption as well as electricity supply. The feed processing stage also had obvious impacts on ADP, AP, GWP, HTP, and POCP. Elec- tric energy consumption was the main source of environment

impact in the feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, and rex rabbit slaughtering stages in each impact category.

In S2, manure treatment mainly contributed to AP, POCP, HTP, ADP, GWP, and EP. Addition of the biogas power generation stage resulted in negative values in each impact category, indicating environmental benefits. The electricity generated in the biogas power generation stage was traced back to the feed processing and rex rabbit breeding stages, which reduced the consumption of external electricity supply and environmental impacts. Therefore, even if S2 increases the industrial structure, the overall environment impacts of S2 are still lower than those of S1.

Figure 3 shows comparison of normalization results for the two scenarios. For S1, TETP was the most significant impact category, followed by FAETP, EP, AP, ADP, GWP, LCP, POCP, HTP, IRP, and ODP. TETP was mainly affected by the consumption of pesticides and chemical fertilizers during the cultivation of feed. While for S2, AP was the most significant impact category, followed by EP, ADP, TETP, FAETP, GWP, LCP, POCP, HTP, IRP, and ODP. In S2, TETP and FAETP were reduced by > 90%, and GWP, HTP, EP, ODP, ADP, and IRP were decreased by 7.2%-31.8%, compared with those in S1. However, AP increased by 119.6% in S2, mainly due to the generation of gases such as SO2 and NO during the manure treatment stage. LCP and POCP hardly changed between the scenarios. Because the value of ODP was too low, it was not analyzed further in our study.
Figure 4 shows the degree of change in S2 when com- pared with S1 with respect to the same life cycle stages and total value. On the basis of the total value, TETP showed

the greatest decrease, and AP, the greatest increase. The decrease of TETP and FAETP in S2 was mainly from the feed cultivation and rex rabbit breeding stages, accounting for > 50%. Because the electric energy generated by biogas was given priority in the rex rabbit breeding and feed pro- cessing stages, GWP, HTP, EP, ODP and IRP decreased to the greatest extent in these two stages. The environmen- tal impact of the rex rabbit breeding stage was zero in S2, mainly because the electricity was recycled in breeding stage (which is closest to the manure treatment and biogas power generation stages) and the outsourced power originally con- sumed in S1 in the breeding stage was replaced. The produc- tion of biogas slurry and slag in the manure treatment stage resulted in a decrease in the use of external fertilizers and insecticides in the feed cultivation stage. The ADP in S2 was reduced by 13.5% when compared with S1, with 68.1% and 85.5% contribution at the feed cultivation stage in S1 and S2, respectively. This was mainly attributable to the envi- ronmental impacts of oil and gas energy consumed in the feed cultivation stage and indirect consumption of electrical energy. Electrical energy supply mainly contributed to ADP in the other stages. However, an obvious increase in AP and POCP was observed in the manure treatment stage, mainly because of the simultaneous production of SO2, NO, and other gases from the biogas, biogas slurry, and slag produced during the manure treatment stage.

These results are consistent with those of previous studies on poultry (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; Pelletier 2008; Silva et al. 2012; Williams et al. 2020). The contribu- tion of feed production to the environmental impacts of a chicken farm was especially high, ranging from 23% (in AP) to 79% (in TETP) (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Bo et al. 2008). Generally, in studies that include the slaugh- tering stage (Bengtsson and Seddon 2013; Katajajuuri 2007; Leinonen et al. 2012; Silva et al. 2012), the contri- bution of this stage to the environmental impacts is mini- mal. However, special attention must be paid to electricity
consumption in the slaughterhouse, as it is particularly high for electric shock production and refrigeration. The following is a detailed analysis of some important indicators.
46: The effects of various production activities on TETP and FAETP were the most significant (Fig. 3). The environmen- tal contributions of TETP and FAETP to S1 were 5.3847 kg 1,4-DB eq and 19.4459 kg 1,4-DB eq, respectively, and to S2 were 0.3515 kg 1,4-DB eq and 1.6357 kg 1,4-DB eq, respectively. In separate analyses, the main environmental contributions of TETP and FAETP were in the feed culti- vation stage. 48: Because the emission reduction targets of UNEP have been strengthened (Alexandratos and Bruinsma 2012), GWP was analyzed separately, taking into account the goal of GWP reduction in S2. The environmental impact of GWP in S2 was reduced by 31.8% compared with S1 In S1, GWP was ranked: feed cultivation stage > feed processing stage > rex rabbit breeding stage > rex rabbit slaughtering stage (Fig. 3). In S2, GWP was ranked: feed cultivation stage > manure treatment stage > feed processing stage > rex rabbit slaugh- tering stage > rex rabbit breeding stage > biogas power gen- eration stage.

In S1, the greater external power consumption led to an increase in GWP in the feed cultivation stage. Electrical energy supply was the main contributor to GWP in the feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, and rex rabbit slaughtering stages.

In S2, as an extension of the well-established circular rex rabbit industry chain, the GWP showed a reduction of 70.9% and 100% in the feed processing and rex rabbit breeding stages compared with that in S1, which is due to the recy- cling of electricity produced in the biogas power generation stage. The biogas power generation stage increased the over- all environmental benefits of the industry chain and reduced the consumption of external electricity power supply in the feed processing and rex rabbit breeding stages, thus reducing the overall GWP impact. The rex rabbit slaughtering stage showed no changes between scenarios.

In S2, GWP was ranked: feed cultivation stage > manure treatment stage > feed processing stage > rex rabbit slaugh- tering stage > rex rabbit breeding stage > biogas power gen- eration stage.

In S1, the greater external power consumption led to an increase in GWP in the feed cultivation stage. Electrical energy supply was the main contributor to GWP in the feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, and rex rabbit slaughtering stages.

In S2, as an extension of the well-established circular rex rabbit industry chain, the GWP showed a reduction of 70.9% and 100% in the feed processing and rex rabbit breeding stages compared with that in S1, which is due to the recy- cling of electricity produced in the biogas power generation stage. The biogas power generation stage increased the over- all environmental benefits of the industry chain and reduced the consumption of external electricity power supply in the feed processing and rex rabbit breeding stages, thus reducing the overall GWP impact. The rex rabbit slaughtering stage showed no changes between scenarios.
50: The AP in S2 increased by 119.6% compared with S1 (Fig. 3), which is mainly reflected in the manure treatment stage; the environmental impact of AP in the manure treat- ment stage was 66.7% in S2. Anaerobic fermentation of manure produces SO2, NO, and other gaseous pollutants

that increase the environmental impact in the manure treat- ment stage, followed by the indirect environmental impact of AP caused by the electrical energy supply. In the feed cultivation stage, the AP of S2 decreased by 17.7% com- pared with S1. In the feed processing and rex rabbit breeding stages, the AP in S2 decreased by 72% and 100% compared with S1. This is mainly because electrical energy supply was the main factor that contributed to AP; S2 recycled the elec- tric energy generated in the biogas power generation stage to reduce external electric energy consumption, significantly reducing the AP value. Although the environmental impact of AP increased during the manure treatment stage, the environmental ben- efits of this stage (production of biogas and biogas slag and slurry) cannot be ignored in the context of the whole life cycle of the rex rabbit. Therefore, S2 is a better breeding model for improved end-waste treatment and byproduct use.
51: The AP in S2 increased by 119.6% compared with S1 (Fig. 3), which is mainly reflected in the manure treatment stage; the environmental impact of AP in the manure treat- ment stage was 66.7% in S2. Anaerobic fermentation of manure produces SO2, NO, and other gaseous pollutants

that increase the environmental impact in the manure treat- ment stage, followed by the indirect environmental impact of AP caused by the electrical energy supply. In the feed cultivation stage, the AP of S2 decreased by 17.7% com- pared with S1. In the feed processing and rex rabbit breeding stages, the AP in S2 decreased by 72% and 100% compared with S1. This is mainly because electrical energy supply was the main factor that contributed to AP; S2 recycled the elec- tric energy generated in the biogas power generation stage to reduce external electric energy consumption, significantly reducing the AP value. 52: In S2, TETP, FAETP, GWP, HTP, EP, ODP, ADP, and IRP showed reductions of 93.8%, 91.7%, 31.8%, 26.5%, 23.2%, 13.8%, 13.5%, and 7.2%, respectively, compared with those of S1 on the basis of the normalization results. By extend- ing the industrial chain, byproducts of the manure treatment stage were reused. The manure was composted and anaero- bically fermented to produce biogas and biogas slurry and slag. The influence of electricity on the production system cannot be ignored. Because of the construction of a circular industry chain, external electricity consumption in S2 was significantly reduced. The electricity generated in the biogas power generation stage was traced back to the industrial chain; the organic fertilizer formed using the biogas slurry and slag was traced back to the feed cultivation stage. This effectively reduced the consumption of external electrical energy and the use of chemical fertilizers and pesticides. Overall, the environmental impacts of the rex rabbit industry chain were significantly reduced and environmental benefits of the enterprise were increased. Considering one rex rab- bit as a functional unit, the annual breeding capacity of the enterprise is 13 million rex rabbits, which can generate an average of 15,210,000 kWh of electricity and 6,240,000 kg of organic fertilizer and provide great economic benefits. Electricity and organic fertilizer can effectively reduce the input costs of the enterprise.

Thus, by establishing a circular rex rabbit industry chain model, the environmental impact of the enterprise can be reduced and environmental benefits can be increased- therefore, the circular rex rabbit industry chain is the better breeding model.
53: In S2, TETP, FAETP, GWP, HTP, EP, ODP, ADP, and IRP showed reductions of 93.8%, 91.7%, 31.8%, 26.5%, 23.2%, 13.8%, 13.5%, and 7.2%, respectively, compared with those of S1 on the basis of the normalization results. The influence of electricity on the production system cannot be ignored. Because of the construction of a circular industry chain, external electricity consumption in S2 was significantly reduced. 54: We have provided a transparent and detailed LCI for tra- ditional and circular rex rabbit industry chains, with feed cultivation, feed processing, rex rabbit breeding, rex rabbit slaughtering, manure treatment, and biogas power generation stages. By performing LCA of the two scenarios, the envi- ronmental feasibility and benefits of the circular rex rabbit industry chain were evaluated and confirmed. From a life cycle perspective, the environmental impacts of most of the stages were decreased in S2 and additional environmental benefits were obtained by reducing the consumption of chem- ical fertilizers and pesticides and external electric power. The feed cultivation stage is the main factor that influences the environmental impact and energy use; however, the environ- mental impact of the circular rex rabbit industry chain in the feed cultivation stage was reduced when compared with the traditional rex rabbit industry chain.

This study confirms that, by perfecting the traditional rex rabbit industry chain and increasing waste reuse and recy- cling to generate clean energy and reduce the consumption of chemical fertilizers and pesticides, we can increase the environmental benefits of rex rabbit breeding and reduce the overall environmental impacts. Therefore, decision makers in rex rabbit breeding enterprises should consider the "cultivating-breeding-processing-waste treatment and reuse" circular industry chain model. This study provides a theoretical and practical reference for optimizing and perfecting the industrial structure for rex rabbit breeding or other breeding industries, thus promoting sustainable development.
